1.description: A simple illustration of the empirical methodology. Consider a firm with the option to borrow from three lenders—Bank A, Bank B, and Bank C—during three periods. Borrowing is denoted by $, intense institutional relationships by solid lines, and intense personal relationships by dashed lines. Between period 1 and period 2, the firm experiences executive turnover, replacing Executive A with Executive B. The firm and Executive A are both endowed with intense (institutional and personal, respectively) relationships with Bank A. Executive B has an intense personal relationship with Bank C, which he gained while working for his former employer. In the first period, the firm borrows from Bank A and Bank B, which means that it maintains its institutional relationship with Bank A and gains one with Bank B in period 2. Thus, in period 1, the personal and institutional relationship effects are not separately identified because the firm and Executive A have the same set of relationships. In period 2, the firm replaces Executive A with Executive B, who has personal relationships with Bank B and Bank C. The firm borrows from Bank B and Bank C in the second period, which means that it maintains its institutional relationship with Bank B and gains an institutional relationship with Bank C in period 3. The difference in terms between the loans from Bank B and Bank C identify the institutional relationship effect in period 2, but there is no within firm variation in personal relationship from which to identify the personal relationship effect. In period 3, the borrower has institutional relationships with all three banks, so there is no within firm variation in institutional relationships with which to identify the institutional relationship effect. However, the firm borrows from Bank A and Bank C, and Executive B does not have a personal relationship with Bank A, so in period 3, the personal relationship effect is identified using the difference in terms between the loans from Bank A and Bank C. If relationships were continuous in this example, then personal and institutional relationships would be separately identified because their intensities would vary over time and across banks.

2.description: Loan spreads around executive turnover events. This figure plots loan spreads against event time, where event time 0 denotes an instance of executive turnover, for firm-bank pairs with high and low institutional relationship intensity. The lines reflect estimates from the regressionSpread(bps)i,j,t=∑τ=−55βτLow1[λi,j,t+τFirm≤0.4616]+∑τ=−55βτHigh1[λi,j,t+τFirm>0.4616]+εi,j,t.The solid line representsβtHighfor τ between −2 and 2 years from executive turnover. The dashed line representsβtLowfor τ between −2 and 2 years from executive turnover. The sample is split into high and lowλi,j,tFirmgroups based on the 75thpercentile of theλi,j,tFirmdistribution, which is 0.4616. Institutional lending relationship intensity is increasing inλi,j,tFirm.

3.description: Investment and personal relationships over time. This figure plotsInvestmenti,t, defined asCAPEXTotalAssetsi,t, for two groups of borrowers. The first group has intense personal lending relationships and the second has weak personal lending relationships. Borrowers are categorized as having intense personal lending relationships ifλi,tExecis above the medianλi,tExecand weak personal lending relationships ifλi,tExecis below the medianλi,tExec. The borrower-quarter sample includes all 928 borrowers and spans 1994 to 2010. SectionV.discusses this figure and TableVIIIpresents the corresponding multivariate regressions.
